text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Public Transparency and Community Trust lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: Public health agencies should be able to explain when AI is used, why it is used, what data are involved, what humans decide, and how concerns can be raised.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
Public health agencies should be able to explain when AI is used, why it is used, what data are involved, what humans decide, and how concerns can be raised.
textPreview: Public health agencies should be able to explain when AI is used, why it is used, what data are involved, what humans decide, and how con...

text: Public trust depends on whether agencies can explain AI use clearly. Transparency should not be a vague notice that AI exists; it should explain purpose, data, human review, limits, safeguards, and feedback options.
textPreview: Public trust depends on whether agencies can explain AI use clearly. Transparency should not be a vague notice that AI exists; it should ...

text: AI use that is invisible or hard to explain can erode trust even when the technical system performs well.
textPreview: AI use that is invisible or hard to explain can erode trust even when the technical system performs well.

text:
Use this slide in the Public Transparency and Community Trust training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 4, 10, 12 and Tools 19, 20, 41.
Emphasize the core guardrail: AI use that is invisible or hard to explain can erode trust even when the technical system performs well.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain Public Transparency and Community Trust in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Public Transparency and Community Trust training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 4, 10, 12 and Tools 19, 20, 41.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

Transparency helps protect public trust. Public health departments should be able to explain when AI is used, why it is used, what data are involved, what humans decide, what safeguards exist, and how people can raise concerns or seek correction. Transparency should be proportional to risk and understandable to the affected audience.

Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 4, 10, 12 and Tools 19, 20, 41.
Ask the group to restate the topic in their own words.

The public health purpose of the AI-supported activity. Whether AI drafts, summarizes, prioritizes, routes, predicts, translates, or recommends. What data types are involved and what data are not used. Who reviews outputs and who makes final.

text: Public AI use notices for community-facing systems. FAQs for staff, partners, and affected communities. Plain-language summaries for governing boards, advisory committees, and public meetings. Internal records in the AI inventory and.
textPreview: Public AI use notices for community-facing systems. FAQs for staff, partners, and affected communities. Plain-language summaries for gove...

text: Be clear about limitations and human oversight rather than overstating AI capability. Avoid vague claims such as 'AI-powered' without explaining what the system actually does. Update notices when use, data, vendor, or risk level changes.

Invite participants to identify where these practices would need to be documented in their own department.
Start with What to Explain, then compare it with the other practice areas on the slide.

text:
Use this slide in the Public Transparency and Community Trust training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 4, 10, 12 and Tools 19, 20, 41.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?
What should staff, partners, and the public know about this AI use? What information can be shared without compromising privacy or security? How will notices be updated when the AI use changes?

text: Draft a plain-language AI use notice for one public-facing workflow. Include purpose, what AI does, what humans decide, data types, safeguards, limitations, and contact or correction options.

Give participants time to work individually or in small groups. Ask them to produce a practical artifact that can feed into readiness assessment, governance review, use case prioritization, or implementation planning.
Expected takeaways include: Plain-language AI use notice, Public FAQ, Feedback and correction process.
Use this topic with Plays 3, 4, 10, 12 and Tools 19, 20, 41. Turn the discussion into a documented question, decision, gap, or implementation artifact.
